join_order.notes: Subquery produces ~15K qualifying orderkeys; orders_orderkey_lookup gives O(1) access
customer_custkey_lookup gives O(1) row access by custkey

data_structures.qualifying_results: flat_array<struct{int32_t orderkey, double sum_qty, int32_t order_row_idx, int32_t cust_row_idx, double o_totalprice, int32_t o_orderdate}>

parallelism.phase1_details: Partition lineitem rows into equal-sized ranges across threads. Align morsel boundaries to orderkey group boundaries using sorted l_orderkey. Each thread scans sequentially and collects qualifying orderkeys locally. Merge results after parallel region (small — ~15K entries).

indexes.purpose: O(1) lookup: qualifying orderkey → orders row index
O(1) lookup: o_custkey → customer row index

optimization_notes: Category A — Plan restructuring. Root cause: data_loading phase (58ms / 117ms total) dominated by MAP_POPULATE forcing synchronous page fault of 913MB across all mmap'd files, including 228MB of orders columns only accessed for ~15K rows. Fix: (1) Remove MAP_POPULATE from ALL mmap calls — for hot runs pages are already in OS page cache, and sequential scan of lineitem naturally triggers hardware prefetch without needing upfront population. (2) Use MADV_SEQUENTIAL for lineitem files (sequential scan), MADV_RANDOM for index/lookup files (sparse random access). (3) Defer mmap of orders and customer columns until after subquery completes — they are only needed for ~15K qualifying rows. (4) Remove the separate data_loading phase entirely — just mmap lazily at point of use. This eliminates the 58ms data_loading overhead; page faults during the scan are near-free on cached pages. Expected total: ~20-30ms.
